data.title: Crypto ETFs: The Latest Wave Of Filings
data.description:
Some of the most interesting filings in the crypto ETF space are not necessarily new product filings. Potential approval of litecoin (LTC-USD) and XRP (XRP-USD) ETFs have stolen the headlines, in addition to some “creative” filings like dogecoin, Trump coin, and other meme coin ETFs. Recently, however, there have also been several filings to make amendments to existing crypto products. These have flown relatively under the radar. They include potential in-kind redemptions in spot crypto ETFs and potential staking in spot ether ETFs.

In-Kind Redemptions in Crypto ETFs

Recently, Nasdaq filed a proposal with the SEC to allow in-kind Bitcoin redemptions in addition to cash redemptions for the iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT). This was followed by a similar filing by the CBOE for the ARK 21Shares Bitcoin ETF (ARKB) and the 21Shares Core Ethereum ETF (CETH). One of the main reasons ETFs are so popular with investors is due to the in-kind creation redemption process. This contributes to their tax efficiency and liquidity. When spot bitcoin ETFs were launched in January 2024, they were approved only for cash redemptions, partly to keep bitcoin out of the hands of authorized participants (AP), due to regulatory concerns. To keep this short, I won’t go into the details of in kind versus cash creation redemption process. For more resources on this process, see these reports by State Street Global Advisors, Charles Schwab Asset Management, and ProShares. Essentially, the cash creation/redemption process adds an extra step of converting between cash and bitcoin with an additional liquidity provider. This way, the AP does not need to “touch” bitcoin.

Grayscale filed a great resource last year - the slide deck discusses some of the benefits of in-kind redemptions, including supporting a deeper, more robust primary market. According to Grayscale, only 15 exchange-traded products are physically-backed commodity funds (not including spot crypto ETFs). All of these use in-kind redemptions. The majority of equity funds also use an in-kind process.

Implications for Investors

It is a common misconception, but in-kind redemptions do not extend to retail investors. So, if you bought and sold shares of IBIT in your brokerage account, you wouldn’t be able to receive any bitcoin in return. Still, this has the potential to create more efficiency and liquidity in the market, as more institutional investors adopt these products. It’s also another step into bringing these crypto ETFs deeper into the mainstream. Last year, IBIT was the third largest ETF by inflows, next to only domestic large-cap heavyweights, the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO) and the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV). In-kind redemptions, along with other characteristics, like recent options trading on select spot bitcoin products, contribute to integrating and aligning these products closer to the financial markets and their ETF counterparts.

Staking in Ether ETFs

Staking was another somewhat controversial proposal during the spot ether ETF launch. It is a way of earning extra rewards by holding ethereum. (This is similar to earning interest or dividends, with a few caveats.) As many ether investors know, staking has become a key feature of ether. It was left out of spot ether ETFs because it resembles unregistered securities offerings. On February 12, the CBOE filed a 19b-4 proposal on the behalf of 21Shares to add staking to its 21Shares Core Ethereum ETF (CETF). Soon after, on February 14, the NYSE filed, on behalf of Grayscale, a proposal to add staking to its two ether products — the Grayscale Ethereum Trust ETF (ETHE) and the Grayscale Ethereum Mini Trust ETF (ETH).

Both filings cite the potential use of “point-and-click” staking as a potentially safer method of earning rewards. This process does not involve the staked ether leaving the wallet, which reduces risk of theft.

Implications for Investors

Staking comes with the potential for additional yield for both retail and institutional clients. Allowing staking in these products more closely aligns the investment with direct ether investment. It also improves efficiency and the creation redemption process. Additionally, this would align the U.S. with other countries which have ethereum staking ETPs — for example, the 3iQ Ether Staking ETF (ETHQ:CA) and the 21Shares Ethereum Staking ETP (AETH).

Bottom Line

Keeping track of new crypto product filings has been exciting — especially with the potential for new spot products like litecoin, XRP, solana, and even several more obscure coins. But there is also a maturation of the current crypto framework supporting products belonging to iShares, Grayscale, and more. That maturation could propel the industry forward in terms of efficiency, liquidity, and reputation.

data.title: Unity Software: Tough Transition Still In The Works
data.description:
Unity Software Inc. (NYSE:U) soared on Q4 results, but the software company didn't report the huge turnaround insinuated by the stock gain. The game engine company is poised for a turnaround, but the business still has a long road ahead. My investment thesis remains Neutral on the stock, with 2025 being another transition year.

Mixed Quarter

Unity Software reported a mixed quarter for Q424, with sales plunging 25% while the company made progress with strategic growth initiatives. The company reported the following Q4 numbers:

The strategic portfolio grew 4% YoY to $442 million, with the Grow Solutions finally growing again. The Grow Solutions sales hit $305 million, up from $299 million in the prior quarter, but still down from the prior peak levels.

The Create Solutions were down nearly 50% in the quarter to $152 million. Strategic portfolio sales were up 9% from last Q4 with subscription revenues up 15% YoY, led by 50% growth in the industry revenue.

The numbers are still very messy with the adjustments back in Q423. In essence, Unity hasn't seen much in the way of growth from the new initiatives, with the turnaround still in the works.

Unity Software did announce a promising move to Vector for the shift of the ad tech business to an updated AI version. In addition, the launch of Unity 6 last year and the end of the runtime fees adjusted the business trajectory already.

On the Q424 earnings call, the CEO made the following comments regarding the Unity Vector product:

Vector is designed to leverage data from across the Unity ecosystem, integrating self-learning artificially intelligence models that will provide deeper insights, optimize performance and deliver better results for customers. Vector enhances targeting precision and increases audience scale through a sharper analysis of richer data sets, and it’s also able to adapt in real time, helping customers navigate an increasingly competitive mobile marketing landscape.

Clearly, the market jumped on the possibility from this opportunity due to the huge gains on AppLovin (APP). The ad tech company has seen the stock gain nearly 700% in the last year.

According to Citi analysts, AppLovin only faces a $40 million revenue headwind from the Unity Software ad tech business. AppLovin reported Q4 revenues of $1.4 billion, far above the current levels of Unity.

The game engine company guided to Q125 revenues of only $405 to $415 million. As with a seasonal business, revenues are down substantially from the $457 million just reported in Q4, with only $15 million in quarterly non-strategic portfolio revenues left. In essence, the vast majority of the revenue dip is related to the strategic portfolio.

Unity Software boosted EBITDA margins back to 23%, hitting $106 million in adjusted EBITDA for the quarter. The company beat internal targets of $83 million and topped the $63 million in adjusted EBITDA reported last Q4 in a positive sign of shoring up the financials of the business.

Tough Transition Ahead

The Q1 outlook was a huge disappointment, with consensus revenue estimates up at $441 million. The stock rally seems to be entirely based on the mention of new AI advertising software, though the shift is a big part of the Q1 weakness.

Unity Software will start the integration and customer shift to the Vector product towards the end of Q1. The company won't complete the move until Q2, and the new AI models will take time to ramp up.

AppLovin has absolutely soared in the last few months due to the excitement about advertising and AI. The stock now trades at nearly 30x forward EV/S targets, versus only 7x for Unity Software when updating for the 30% gain.

While Unity has a huge market opportunity, the Vector AI product only transitions at the end of Q1. The company doesn't forecast any material benefits in the near term, as evidenced by the weak guidance for the March quarter.

While the 2025 numbers still include some headwinds from the exited business, analysts only forecast flat sales, prior to the weak guidance. The consensus analyst estimates only forecast 2026 revenue growth of ~9% to reach just $1.94 billion.

Unity Software reported a vastly improved quarter with a turn in the strategic portfolio, but the company still has a long way to go to return to strong growth to warrant a higher stock price beyond the 30% run following Q4 earnings. The company remains a leader in game engine development software. Unity is moving into the XR/AR segments with work on the Android platform and with Metaquest, but those areas are still very small.

Investors should be for a better entry point after weak Q1 numbers and Q2 guidance pulls the stock back down.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Unity Software is already priced for the business reporting solid growth. The company still faces a lot of headwinds and the AI ad tech software will impact revenues as much as help in the 1H of the year.

Investors should look to buy the stock on weakness after the rally to $28.

data.description:
Market review

The fourth quarter of 2024 marked a strong finish to an impressive year for U.S. equities. The broader economy demonstrated resilience, supported by steady inflation, consumer spending, and earnings growth that largely exceeded expectations. Artificial intelligence emerged as a pivotal driver for growth with companies investing heavily to capitalize on its potential. The S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) advanced 2.41% for the quarter and 25.02% for the year. Growth stocks maintained their leadership position for the quarter and the year, as noted by the Russell 1000 Growth Index's 33.36% annual return. While mid- and small-cap stocks experienced periods of favorable leadership during the year, they ultimately lagged their large-cap counterparts.

Driving the quarterly returns was an initial bullish reaction to the U.S. presidential election. Investors responded optimistically to the potential for business-friendly policies and faster economic growth. Following the election, there was an immediate rotation into cyclicals, small-cap stocks, and speculative investments such as cryptocurrencies and lower-quality equities.

The postelection fervor wore off later in the quarter as investors realized that key aspects of the Trump policy agenda could create inflationary pressures. When gross domestic product and inflation came in higher than expected, the U.S. Federal Reserve signaled fewer rate cuts than previously anticipated. A “higher-for-longer” sentiment drove the term premium for the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield (US10Y) to rise 75 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) during the quarter. As 2024 came to a close, the bullish enthusiasm faltered over the prospects of rising inflation, higher interest rates, and falling stock prices.

Portfolio Positioning: Quarterly Attribution and Analysis

Security selection within financials and IT contributed positively to performance. AppLovin Corp. (APP) built a software platform that enables developers to monetize mobile apps. With the stock price appreciating significantly during the quarter, we modestly trimmed the position. Robinhood Markets, Inc. We hold conviction in Robinhood's ability to capture market share and deliver long-term growth for investors.

While the overall IT sector contributed positively to performance, select IT holdings significantly detracted from returns. Broadcom Inc. (AVGO), a leading provider of semiconductor and software products, holds more than one-third of the market share within application-specific integrated circuits (ASICs). During the quarter, we initiated a position within Broadcom, yet the relative underweight to the benchmark detracted from returns. We will continue to opportunistically increase the position within Broadcom. Monolithic Power Systems, Inc. (MPWR), specializes in power management solutions for semiconductor chips. Monolithic exceeded expectations and raised guidance; however, shares sharply sold off over the threat of increased competition. We believe Monolithic's notably faster growth and innovative technology should allow the company to remain an industry leader.

The fund outperformed the benchmark over the trailing 12-month period, in part due to security selection within IT and industrials. Arista Networks, Inc. Portfolio Positioning: Outlook

The rising interest rate backdrop created a pause in the broadening of market leadership. While a few speculative corners of markets, such as cryptocurrencies and meme stocks, showed momentum, U.S. equities took a generally defensive turn during the quarter. Mega-cap tech stocks resumed market leadership while small caps and more cyclical and rate-sensitive sectors lagged. Our response to this evolving environment was to remain disciplined.

While policy shifts may trigger short-term rotations, we believe that structural growth scarcity should continue to command premium valuations for high-quality growth companies. We avoid chasing cyclical rallies under the banner of "new regimes" and instead keep our focus on businesses with sustainable earnings growth and strong competitive positions. We believe that security selection—not macroeconomic timing—should drive performance in 2025.

We remain excited about the technological innovation inherent in our portfolio companies. We own businesses with durable competitive advantages at the forefront of driving productivity gains. AI spending should remain a key growth driver, potentially ushering in a super cycle of investment that transcends the IT sector. We expect the next phase of AI should shift from building capacity to practical applications in 2025. Our investment process is designed to capture the strong growth potential of these trends.

data.title: The London Company Income Equity Vs. Russell 1000 Value Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market Update

U.S. equities traded higher during 4Q, with most of the major indices posting positive gains. Economic data released during the quarter were positive, but choppy. The Fed's monetary policy continued on a less restrictive path, but shifted a bit more hawkish in December, reflecting higher than desired inflation, a strong labor market and better-than-expected GDP growth in recent quarters. The more hawkish view from the Fed led investors to assume fewer rate cuts in the months ahead. The broader market, measured by the Russell 3000 Index, rose 2.6%. Similar to earlier in the year, larger companies with attractive growth profiles led the market. Looking at market factors, Growth, Volatility, and Momentum posted the strongest returns, while Value, Yield, and most Quality factors presented headwinds.

Key Performance Takeaways

  • The London Company Income Equity portfolio declined 2.2% (-2.3% net) during the quarter vs. a 2.0% decrease in the Russell 1000 Value Index. Stock selection was as headwind to relative performance, partially offset by positive sector exposure.

  • The Income Equity portfolio slightly trailed the Russell 1000 Value in 4Q and fell short of our 75-80% downside capture expectations. Dividend yield headwinds picked up at the end of the quarter, as long-term rates increased. Further, we received limited benefit from our Quality exposure.

  • We continue to believe that Quality factors will add value over full cycles. Our focus on high returns on capital, balance sheet strength, and attractive shareholder yield reinforces our margin of safety and positions our Income Equity portfolio for success in this uncertain climate.

Top 3 Contributors to Relative Performance

  • Apple Inc. (AAPL) - AAPL shares posted solid gains during 4Q after the company released its long-awaited AI product, which kept alive the potential for an iPhone super cycle. R&D remains strong at AAPL, which should lead to solid revenue growth and significant cash flow generation. The company's large installed base is an important long-term driver of the business.

  • Charles Schwab Corporation (SCHW) - Shares of SCHW outperformed as cash sorting issue continues to normalize. Core net new assets also improved/normalized, following the end of the Ameritrade migration. We have conviction that the stock has further room to run over the next 12-18 months as the company's real earnings power and growth potential become evident.

  • BlackRock, Inc. (BLK) - BLK shares outperformed in 4Q reflecting improving organic base fee growth as well as positive market performance. We continue to believe that BLK is positioned well for long-term market share growth through its iShares ETF business and its growing presence in private markets. We also continue to view BLK as a strong fit to our process with its low leverage, growing dividend, and steady repurchase of shares.

Top 3 Detractors from Relative Performance

  • Crown Castle Inc. (CCI) - CCI underperformed during 4Q driven by the lower-than-expected rumored valuation for a potential sale of the fiber/small cell businesses, and slower interest rate cuts in 2025. CCI reported positive tower activity, but canceled some low-return small cell projects, which was viewed as a negative this quarter. The new management team has taken action to improve the return profile of the business and margins have already shown improvement. CCI is in a good position for future growth given its tower locations and U.S.-focused portfolio. We like CCI's stable revenue stream, long-term tailwinds on growth in data consumption, and its ability to return cash to shareholders through its dividend policy.

  • Nestle S.A. (OTCPK:NSRGY) - NSRGY shares underperformed the broader market all year. Sentiment across the packaged food space is low as it emerges from two years of unprecedented food price inflation. NSRGY's latest earnings report did not suggest an acceleration in organic growth from current subdued trends. NSRGY's portfolio is attractively positioned in categories that have stable, long-term volume tailwinds such as coffee, pet food, and nutritional health. Barring further executional missteps, we believe the downside to the current stock price is low.

  • Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK) - MRK continues to deliver solid growth in its blockbuster immunotherapy treatment, Keytruda, as well as encouraging contributions from new launches. However, investor sentiment was negatively impacted by a weaker outlook for its HPV vaccine due to challenges in China and broader pharma sector underperformance following the election and health care-related nominations, as the market assesses potential policy implications from the incoming administration. These concerns appear overstated, and we remain confident that MRK's strong portfolio of drugs and vaccines coupled with its effective leadership will create long-term value for shareholders.

Sector Influence

We are bottom-up stock pickers, but sector exposures influenced relative performance as follows:

  • What Helped: Underweight Health Care (a weaker performing sector) & overweight Info. Technology (a better performing sector)

  • What Hurt: Overweight Materials & Consumer Staples (weaker performing sectors)

Trades During the Quarter

  • Increased: Dominion Energy (D) - Addition reflects our growing confidence in the stability of future earnings following negotiations with some of the state Utilities commissions, as well as the sale of some of the non-regulated utilities businesses.

Looking Ahead

As we enter 2025, we believe the market faces an inflection point where sustaining momentum becomes increasingly difficult. Across the real economy, demand still seems sluggish and clear late-cycle signals persist. Revenue growth and corporate profits have leaned on inflationary pricing, but margins face growing headwinds as inflationary pricing fades, input costs rise, and demand softens. The Fed cut rates during 2024, but the yields on longer-dated treasuries actually rose as the year ended. Stubbornly high borrowing costs continue to plague rate-sensitive areas of the economy, like housing. Employment and inflation data may be volatile in 2025 and could affect changes in monetary policy and lead to greater volatility across equity markets.

Despite resilient economic data and limited signs of credit risk, we believe vigilance is warranted. Our cautious posture persists due to high valuations, market concentration, looming debt challenges, and fraying consumer health. We anticipate lower expected returns in the near term, based on slowing growth (function of restrictive monetary policy) and high valuations. Valuation multiple expansion can only take the market so far (particularly late in a market cycle). We expect a reversion to the mean whereby earnings growth & dividends drive returns going forward. While optimism remains high, the vulnerabilities of momentum-driven leadership highlight the need for discipline. Markets may reward risk-taking in the short term, but lasting wealth is built through patience, real income, and fundamentals.

This strategy has a more conservative orientation, with a focus on capital preservation, income and growth, in order to provide greater yield and downside protection relative to our Large and Mid Cap strategies. Our Income Equity strategy is designed to generate above-average, absolute returns over full market cycles. Accounts in this product composite are fully discretionary taxable and tax-exempt portfolios with no minimum dollar amount of assets. The product is measured against the Russell 1000 Value Index and has a creation and inception date of December 31, 1999. There is no use of leverage, derivatives or short positions. All actual fee-paying discretionary portfolios are included in one or more composites that have been managed for a full calendar quarter with limited restrictions and similar objectives. As of July 1, 2022 The London Company redefined the composites to exclude all dual contract relationships and any potentially bundled fee scenarios. This policy is not retroactive, but will continue to apply going forward.

Benchmark Description: The Russell 1000 Value Index measures the performance of the large-cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 1000 Index companies with lower price-to-book ratios and lower expected growth values. Benchmark returns are not covered by the report of independent verifiers.

Performance and Fees: Gross of fee returns are calculated gross of management and custodian fees and net of transaction costs. Net of fee returns are calculated net of actual London Company management fees and transaction costs and gross of custodian and other fees. Returns may be net of miscellaneous fund expenses. The gross figures do not reflect the deduction of investment advisory fees. Returns are calculated and stated in U.S. dollars. Prior to April 1, 2024 returns are calculated gross of withholding taxes on foreign dividends and interest. Starting April 1, 2024, performance is calculated net or gross of foreign withholding taxes on dividends and interest income dependent on custodian data. Dividends are reinvested. The accounts represented in the composite reflect actual London Company fees paid on the particular account which may be different from the fee normally offered to other clients. Policies for valuing investments, calculating performance, and preparing GIPS Reports are available upon request.

data.title: Allspring Mid Cap Growth Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

The fourth quarter of 2024 marked a strong finish to an impressive year for U.S. equities. The broader economy demonstrated resilience, supported by steady inflation, consumer spending, and earnings growth that largely exceeded expectations. Artificial intelligence emerged as a pivotal driver for growth with companies investing heavily to capitalize on its potential. The S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) advanced 2.41% for the quarter and 25.02% for the year. Growth stocks maintained their leadership position for the quarter and the year, as noted by the Russell 1000 Growth Index's 33.36% annual return. While mid- and small-cap stocks experienced periods of favorable leadership during the year, they ultimately lagged their large-cap counterparts.

Driving the quarterly returns was an initial bullish reaction to the U.S. presidential election. Investors responded optimistically to the potential for business-friendly policies and faster economic growth. Following the election, there was an immediate rotation into cyclicals, small-cap stocks, and speculative investments such as cryptocurrencies and lower-quality equities.

The postelection fervor wore off later in the quarter as investors realized that key aspects of the Trump policy agenda could create inflationary pressures. When gross domestic product and inflation came in higher than expected, the U.S. Federal Reserve signaled fewer rate cuts than previously anticipated. A “higher-for-longer” sentiment drove the term premium for the 10-year U.S. Treasury (US10Y) yield to rise 75 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) during the quarter. As 2024 came to a close, the bullish enthusiasm faltered over the prospects of rising inflation, higher interest rates, and falling stock prices.

Portfolio Positioning: Outlook

The rising interest rate backdrop created a pause in the broadening of market leadership. While a few speculative corners of markets, such as cryptocurrencies and meme stocks, showed momentum, U.S. equities took a generally defensive turn during the quarter. Mega-cap tech stocks resumed market leadership while small caps and more cyclical and rate-sensitive sectors lagged. Our response to this evolving environment was to remain disciplined.

While policy shifts may trigger short-term rotations, we believe that structural growth scarcity should continue to command premium valuations for high-quality growth companies. We avoid chasing cyclical rallies under the banner of "new regimes" and instead keep our focus on businesses with sustainable earnings growth and strong competitive positions. We believe that security selection—not macroeconomic timing—should drive performance in 2025.

We remain excited about the technological innovation inherent in our portfolio companies. We own businesses with durable competitive advantages at the forefront of driving productivity gains. AI spending should remain a key growth driver, potentially ushering in a super cycle of investment that transcends the IT sector. We expect the next phase of AI should shift from building capacity to practical applications in 2025. Our investment process is designed to capture the strong growth potential of these trends.

data.title: If I Had To Retire Today, These Are The Dividend Stocks I'd Bet My Future On
data.description:
Introduction

On Feb. 14, I wrote an article covering my entire dividend growth portfolio, which triggered a lot of comments (thank you for all the support!).

One of the things many readers brought up is the fact that my portfolio yields less than 2.0%. That's the result of my age (I'm 29) and my focus on dividend growth over income, as I'm still in the wealth-building phase of my retirement journey.

Nonetheless, I own some higher-yielding stocks because I believe "value" stocks have a great chance of outperforming "growth" in the years ahead, supported by attractive valuation benefits and unfavorable macroeconomic developments like "higher for longer" inflation and interest rates.

In this article, we won't discuss these developments. We'll do something different, as I promised many readers in recent weeks to write an article with stocks that I would buy if I were focusing on income.

Or, to put it differently, what stocks would I own if I were to retire right now?

So, let's keep this intro short - for once - and get right to it!

How Much Do We Need?

As I have written in prior articles, I have been very blessed as I was able to use the past few years to build a decent portfolio with a mid-to-high six-figure net worth that could - technically - make me a millionaire way before the legal retirement age in most Western nations.

While I cannot imagine retiring, as I love my job a lot, I have spent a lot of time thinking about the possibilities of retirement, as requirements vary a lot.

For example, Fidelity wrote an article titled "How much do I need to retire?"

As you can imagine, there's no single answer, just a bunch of guidelines that may or may not apply to your individual situation. These guidelines include the paragraph below (emphasis added):

Our savings factors are based on the assumption that a person saves 15% of their income annually beginning at age 25 (which includes any employer match), invests more than 50% on average of their savings in stocks over their lifetime, retires at age 67, and plans to maintain their preretirement lifestyle in retirement (see footnote 1 for more details). - Fidelity

If you follow these rules, you should be able to save 10x your pre-retirement income by age 67, which can help you maintain your pre-retirement lifestyle during retirement.

So, what if you're late?

  • If you're below the age of 40, the general consensus is that you save more money and invest for growth.
  • If you're over the age of 40, you may have to make more sacrifices, as you cannot benefit from "time in the market" as much as younger people. This includes a combination of higher savings, reduced spending, and potentially a retirement delay (if possible).

The bottom line, however, is to simply invest. That's the one piece of advice that covers all ages, genders, races, nationalities, you name it.

After all, even an investment of $200 per month without an initial investment turns into nearly $140,000 after 20 years when compounded at 10% per year.

Sure, $140,000 won't make you rich or give you that fancy lifestyle one reads about in magazines, but it sure makes a huge difference for many.

With that said, if we completely ignore Social Security and other sources of income, it's always interesting to see what kind of income one needs to retire.

The map below is based on SmartAsset data that calculates the income needed to cover a 50/30/20 budget, where 50% of earnings are allocated to necessities, 30% to discretionary spending, and 20% to savings.

The data, which is as of Feb. 14, shows that in California, one needs $114,000 to live comfortably. In West Virginia, that number is $79,000.

If we assume we can buy 5% income without taking on elevated risks, we would need $1.6 million to retire in West Virginia. Again, this ignores all other sources of income and taxes. In California, one needs $2.3 million.

In Europe, the situation is a bit different.

As reported by Yahoo Finance, there are quite a bit of places where retirement is possible with a lot less capital. I added emphasis to the quote below:

The capital city of Spain is famous for its royal palaces, sprawling parks and amazing street food. Though enchanting, Madrid is not a particularly affordable city. The estimated monthly cost for a single person is 2,151 euros, or about $2,309.

[...] Athens is brimming with cultural wonders. This ancient city has a welcoming vibe, making it an especially inviting place for expats. For one person, the estimated monthly cost of living in Athens is 1,609 euros, or about $1,727.

[...] Situated along the Danube River, Hungary’s capital city is a treasure trove of wonders. You’ll find magnificent castles, exciting festivals and tons of spas boasting thermal baths. This is one of the more affordable places to hang your hat. A single person’s estimated monthly cost of living is 540,925 forint, or about $1,470.

If we apply the same 5% yield, one needs less than $400,000 to retire in Hungary's capital city. Obviously, as I have said over and over again, I'm not retiring, but it's fun to know that if I wanted, I could move to a lot of European nations.

It also helps me to think about suitable investments, as someone who chooses to retire early will - more than likely - be dependent on his own income sources, which requires top-tier dividend stocks that come with income, stability, and inflation protection.

Also, the goal is to get an average yield of at least 5%.

In the next part of this article, I'll present some of the stocks that I would buy right now if I were to move to a place like Hungary.

Low-Risk Energy Income: TC Energy (TRP) & Antero Midstream (AM)

Extra note: both TRP and AM are C-Corps. They do NOT issue K-1 forms.

I love midstream. In an article published on Jan. 15, I called it "my single favorite place to buy income."

Midstream companies own the transportation, storage, and processing assets of oil and gas producers, which means revenues are mostly based on fixed fees, massively reducing commodity price risks.

Due to rapid growth in oil and gas production, the US is in desperate need of more pipelines. This includes the mighty Permian Basin. Moreover, because of a bigger focus on exports, a lot more pipelines for natural gas, natural gas liquids, and refined products are needed.

That's where TC Energy and Antero Midstream come in.

Antero Midstream is a holding in my portfolio with a yield of 5.4%. It owns the midstream assets of Antero Resources (AR) in Appalachia. Going into this year, it achieved its leverage ratio target of 3.0x.

Although the company's focus on the Marcellus Shale somewhat limits growth, its small size and relationship with Antero Resources are fantastic drivers of high visibility and very efficient capital investments.

Since 2014, the company has grown its adjusted EBITDA by 17% per year and raised its return on invested capital to 19%, one of the best numbers in the industry.

Although the company is unlikely to hike its dividend this year, elevated free cash flow is expected to allow it to aggressively buy back stock and pave the way for consistent long-term dividend growth. This is what I wrote in a recent article that included the AM ticker:

  • The 5.4% dividend is super safe, protected by a very healthy payout ratio and very stable revenues.
  • An implied 8.9% free cash flow yield implies that a value equivalent to 3.5% of its market cap can be used to buy back stock and reduce debt this year.
  • Based on its free cash flow yield, the potential total shareholder return yield is 8.9%.
  • Next year, analysts expect $760 million in free cash flow, 7% more than in 2025E and nearly 10% of its $8.0 billion market cap.

With that said, for more consistent dividend growth, I would add TC Energy, the Canadian natural gas giant. It's the only midstream company connecting all three North American nations through a pipeline network of 93,000 kilometers (58,000 miles) and nuclear power generation of 6.4 megawatts. Last year, it spun off South Bow (SOBO), which made it a pure-play natural gas midstream company.

Whereas Antero Midstream's revenues are highly tied to Antero Resources' operations, TC Energy benefits more from "big picture" developments. This includes expectations of 40 billion cubic feet per day in additional North American natural gas demand by 2035 (to 160 Bcf/d). It also has a 30% market share in liquified natural gas shipments, a market that's expected to quadruple between 2023 and 2035.

On top of that, TC Energy's stability is nearly unmatched as it generates 90% of its revenue from natural gas with close to 100% of its cash coming from take-or-pay/regulated deals.

Even if some of its pipelines aren't used, fees will still apply, which adds tremendous protection during times of weak energy prices.

This is visible in its dividends as the dividend has been hiked for 24 consecutive years (spin-off adjusted). This includes the Great Financial Crisis, the 2014/2015 oil crash, and the pandemic.

Currently yielding 5.0%, the company aims to grow its dividend by 3-5% per year going forward, a number that provides decent inflation protection.

Combined, AM and TRP provide me with 5.2% income and low-single-digit long-term dividend growth, assuming that AM's dividend growth will be similar to TRP's growth profile.

6% Net Least Income: Realty Income (O) And VICI Properties (VICI)

When it comes to buying income, it's hard to ignore the triple net lease giants Realty Income and VICI Properties. Triple net lease deals mean that their tenants pay for insurance, taxes, and maintenance. While this often leads to lower base rent, it also reduces a lot of complexities for the landlords.

Realty Income is the biggest net lease company on the plant with a 5.7% dividend yield, 30 consecutive years of dividend growth (4.3% CAGR), and close to 15,500 properties. It also enjoys an A-range credit rating.

Moreover, the overwhelming majority of its tenants operate in defensive industries with protection against e-commerce disruption. This includes grocery stores, convenience stores, dollar stores, home improvement, quick-service restaurants, and drug stores - among others.

While the market is worried about the future of Walgreens (WBA) and CVS (CVS), drug stores account for slightly less than 5.0% of total annual rent. Moreover, the retail real estate market is strong enough to facilitate a slow move away from risky tenants.

In general, the company has done a great job maintaining an above-average occupancy rate and consistently adjusted funds from operations ("AFFO") growth. Currently, the company has a 98.2% occupancy rate and 3-5% average annual per-share AFFO growth expectations (FactSet).

Since the 1990s, the company has consistently grown AFFO - regardless of the interest rate environment, as we can see below.

With that said, the "problem" in real estate is that it's hard to establish a moat. After all, capital is often the only thing keeping competitors at bay.

VICI Properties, however, has figured out how to create a moat as it's a leisure/entertainment-focused REIT that generates roughly half of its revenues in Las Vegas - or on the Las Vegas Strip, to be more precise.

In 2017, the company was founded as a result of the Caesars Entertainment bankruptcy, which led to the spin-off of major real estate assets. In 2021, it bought MGM Growth Properties, which made it an even bigger player.

More than 80% of its annual rent is generated from three tenants:

  • Caesars Entertainment: 39%
  • MGM Resorts (MGM): 35%
  • The Venetian Las Vegas: 9%

Even better than relying on the strongest players in gaming, the company's assets are highly differentiated as it owns assets like MGM Grand, The Venetian, Mandalay Bay, Caesars Palace, Park MGM, and others.

These assets have lease terms of more than 40 years and have 100% rent collection. That was even the case during the pandemic when these assets were shut down.

With regard to its dividend, the company yields 5.7%. This dividend has grown by 7.0% per year since its 2018 IPO. Going forward, I expect 4-6% average annual dividend growth, which would be in line with per-share AFFO growth expectations (FactSet).

Moreover, after I disliked deals like the investment in bowling alleys, I was very happy to see the company partnering with luxury real estate giants in places like Los Angeles. This includes One Beverly Hills. I believe this is a fantastic market to be in, especially because it comes with higher barriers to entry.

While VICI may be boring, it's the right kind of boring I want to own when the focus is on income.

I Cannot Say No To Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ)

Usually, I avoid putting more than one Canadian stock in an article. That's not because I dislike the Canadians (I don't) but because most of my readers are Americans who like to stick to domestic stocks, which is perfectly fine.

Nonetheless, when it comes to buying income with inflation protection, I have to include Canadian Natural as it's my favorite oil and gas producer with a fantastic dividend growth profile and a wide range of other benefits.

Canadian Natural is Canada's largest oil producer and second-largest natural gas producer. It also has the second-biggest reserves (relative to its market cap) among the world's biggest oil producers. It's the only Canadian player with more than 5 billion barrels of oil equivalent in proven reserves.

Most of these reserves are in the mighty Western Canadian Sedimentary Basin, one of the most prolific oil basins anywhere in the world. Its total reserves translate to a reserve life of roughly 33 years, which the company is keeping elevated through new discoveries.

Because most operations are in oil sands and thermal, the company has very low decline rates. US onshore producers, for example, have elevated decline rates, as consistent investments are needed to keep up conventional onshore drilling. Oil sands have high initial costs due to expensive mining operations. However, maintaining these operations is attractive.

Even better, because of plentiful reserves, the company has a lot of room to grow production. Since 2001, the company has grown its per-share production by 9%, supported by buybacks. For 2025, it aims for at least 12% per-share production growth, fueled by the takeover of Chevron's (CVX) oil sand assets.

With regard to its dividend, the company has hiked its dividend for 25 consecutive years. In 4Q24, the company hiked its dividend by 7.1%. Over the past 25 years, the dividend has grown by 21% per year. In 2022, when oil prices were elevated, it paid a special dividend.

It currently has a base yield of 5.2%.

To give you an idea of how much return potential this company has, at $75 WTI, it generates more than $5 in per-share free cash flow. That's 12% of its current market cap, one of the best numbers in the entire industry - excluding some smaller players. At $85 WTI, that number rises to 16%.

Although CNQ is currently lowering debt after buying Chevron's assets, I expect the company to return 100% of its free cash flow in 2026.

Please note that I'm not worried about tariffs. The U.S. needs Canadian oil and the elevated trade war risks could finally cause Canada to focus more on domestic pipelines to access new non-U.S. export markets. Hence, on a long-term basis, I believe these trade risks could even be bullish, as export diversification could translate to higher margins.

All things considered, while CNQ comes with oil and gas pricing risks, I love its growth profile, inflation protection, and ability to consistently return cash to shareholders through (special) dividends and aggressive buybacks.

I currently own CNQ and will buy a lot more if I ever decide to retire.

Takeaway

If I were to retire today, my income-focused portfolio would look very different from my current dividend growth strategy. At 29, I prioritize long-term wealth building, but imagining retirement has been a fun exercise.

I would target a >5% yield, focusing on stability, inflation protection, and consistent income.

Stocks like TC Energy and Antero Midstream offer reliable midstream energy income, while Realty Income and VICI Properties provide defensive real estate exposure with strong dividends.

Meanwhile, Canadian Natural Resources would also make the cut for its impressive reserves, growth potential, and inflation-beating returns.

While I'm not retiring anytime soon, it's reassuring to know that with the right investments, financial independence is achievable - whether in California, West Virginia, or even Budapest.

So, what's the takeaway?

Start investing early, stay consistent, and let compounding work its magic.

Risks To My Thesis

Although Midstream companies are protected from commodity price swings due to fixed-fee contracts, they could face regulatory and operational challenges.

REITs like Realty Income and VICI Properties are vulnerable to tenant defaults or shifts in consumer behavior. Canadian Natural Resources is exposed to oil price volatility.

All stocks are prone to recession and general stock market correction risks.

data.description:
Market review

The fourth quarter of 2024 marked a strong finish to an impressive year for U.S. equities. The broader economy demonstrated resilience, supported by steady inflation, consumer spending, and earnings growth that largely exceeded expectations. Artificial intelligence emerged as a pivotal driver for growth with companies investing heavily to capitalize on its potential. The S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) advanced 2.41% for the quarter and 25.02% for the year. Growth stocks maintained their leadership position for the quarter and the year, as noted by the Russell 1000 Growth Index's 33.36% annual return. While mid- and small-cap stocks experienced periods of favorable leadership during the year, they ultimately lagged their large-cap counterparts.

Driving the quarterly returns was an initial bullish reaction to the U.S. presidential election. Investors responded optimistically to the potential for business-friendly policies and faster economic growth. Following the election, there was an immediate rotation into cyclicals, small-cap stocks, and speculative investments such as cryptocurrencies and lower-quality equities.

The post-election fervor wore off later in the quarter as investors realized that key aspects of the Trump policy agenda could create inflationary pressures. When gross domestic product and inflation came in higher than expected, the U.S. Federal Reserve signaled fewer rate cuts than previously anticipated. A “higher-for-longer” sentiment drove the term premium for the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield to rise 75 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) during the quarter. As 2024 came to a close, the bullish enthusiasm faltered over the prospects of rising inflation, higher interest rates, and falling stock prices.

Portfolio Positioning: Outlook

The rising interest rate backdrop created a pause in the broadening of market leadership. While a few speculative corners of markets, such as cryptocurrencies and meme stocks, showed momentum, U.S. equities took a generally defensive turn during the quarter. Mega-cap tech stocks resumed market leadership while small caps and more cyclical and rate-sensitive sectors lagged. Our response to this evolving environment was to remain disciplined.

While policy shifts may trigger short-term rotations, we believe that structural growth scarcity should continue to command premium valuations for high-quality growth companies. We avoid chasing cyclical rallies under the banner of "new regimes" and instead keep our focus on businesses with sustainable earnings growth and strong competitive positions. We believe that security selection—not macroeconomic timing—should drive performance in 2025.

We remain excited about the technological innovation inherent in our portfolio companies. We own businesses with durable competitive advantages at the forefront of driving productivity gains. AI spending should remain a key growth driver, potentially ushering in a super cycle of investment that transcends the IT sector. We expect the next phase of AI should shift from building capacity to practical applications in 2025. Our investment process is designed to capture the strong growth potential of these trends.

data.title: Is It Time To Invest In Europe?
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Going abroad

There has been a lot of pessimism about Europe, given stalling growth, rising costs, tariff threats and the war in Ukraine. Former economic engine Germany has stagnated for two consecutive years and things don't look much better in France, Italy, or elsewhere. Interestingly, European stock markets have performed well as of late, even beating the U.S. since the beginning of the year.

Snapshot: Increased military spending pushed the region's defense stocks to all-time highs on Monday, while the STOXX Europe 600 Index (STOXX) has already climbed 8.6% in 2025, more than double the 4% return of the S&P 500 (SP500). Things look even better for the Euro Stoxx 50 Index (SX5E), which is up more than 14% YTD, and even for France and Germany, where the CAC 40 (CAC:IND) and DAX (DAX:IND) are ahead by 10.4% and 12.4% YTD. This bullishness has surprised many, especially when many nations in Europe are mired in political volatility.

What's going on? European central banks are expected to continue easing as the Federal Reserve stays on pause, while Ukraine ceasefire talks are underway, as well as a push toward stimulus in prior fiscally disciplined countries. European markets are also way smaller than the U.S., so even a little bit of diversification money being transferred there will show an outsized impact. At the recent World Economic Forum, BlackRock's (BLK) Larry Fink called investment in Europe a counter-trade, saying, "Always go against Davos and you'll make a lot of money."

Elsewhere: Germans are going to the polls this weekend, with the CDU's Friedrich Merz poised to be the next chancellor, though he may face a difficult time forming a governing majority due to the rise of Alternative for Germany (AfD). On the ballot are stances over migration, as well as economic malaise prompted by the loss of industry and cheap Russian energy. For investors, there also might be more of a spotlight on reforms to the Schuldenbremse, or debt brake, to finance structural investments for the economy. In terms of foreign markets, Seeking Alpha subscribers actually see more opportunity in India compared to other regions for investment, according to the latest SA Sentiment Survey, though Europe came in second place. Will the gains last?

What else is happening...

007 takeover: Amazon (AMZN) to make future James Bond films.

Citigroup (C) drops diversity goals, 'DEI' term after Trump order.

Beverage jolt: Celsius (CELH) skyrockets on Alani Nu deal.

data.title: Tel-Aviv Stock Exchange: An Enticing Moat-Rich Stock Market Operator To Own For Life
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Oliver Mitchell as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Image source: Seeking Alpha suggestion

Before we get started

Currency is in Shekels unless otherwise specified

Links are to the relevant section of their filing, for the sake of ease and due diligence

If you are reading at least a quarter after this article's publication date, make sure to check the recent figures.

Summary

The Tel-Aviv Stock Exchange (OTCPK:TVAVF) (TASE.TA), a 4.4bn mkt cap enterprise, presents an opportunity as an undervalued business with a considerable moat, both on a quantitative and qualitative basis, with very few risks attached. They are a business you could own forever – value creation will continue indefinitely as measured by FCF growth over time (the only driver of stock prices in the long run). The opportunity exists due to the market, once again, overlooking what’s right in front of them. This will all be in more detail in what’s coming, but just so you get the idea.

Note: Links are to the relevant section of their filing, for the sake of ease and DD.

The Business

Business Description

The Tel Aviv Stock exchange (henceforth TASE) is an Israel-based/listed business who manage the only stock exchange in Israel. As a stock market operator, their primary concern day to day is approving the issuance of activities (listings) and providing the relevant data to firms already listed.

Their subsidiaries, as per Their Last Report, are as follows:

TASE Clearing House: the clearing and settlement of their securities (excluding derivatives), meaning they make sure all relevant amounts are paid to the right parties, and that the orders are filled at the appropriate times. Derivatives are far too complex to be worth the trouble, which shows a sound perspective on the side of the management.

MAOF Clearing House: this is where they handle the issuance of options and futures which they excluded from their other subsidiaries’ activities. Their responsibilities are the same as the TASE clearing house, just for different securities, so I’ll avoid repeating myself.

They also act as a Central Securities Depository: they are responsible for holding and managing marketable securities. While it is counterintuitive, it is standard practice as it makes sure ownership is transferred in a proper manner.

The Moat

TASE’s moat is both wide and deep – competitors may try to cross it, but they’re likely to exhaust themselves along the way. This afforded them a lasting competitive advantage:

A legally enforced monopoly within their niche: They are the only stock exchange in Israel. Their markets are unusual in the sense that the listing requirements are extremely low, unlike e.g. the NYSE’s $100mn mkt cap and 3yr earnings min. $10mn requirement, who businesses may choose to IPO on via ADR instead (which has been known to happen incredibly often). This has allowed the TASE market to become comprised of both smaller businesses in need of equity financing along with larger ones. Identical exchanges may come along, but the Israeli regulation is such that it takes years for any competitor to gain their footing. Obviously, TASE aren’t going to lower their drawbridge – they have the reputation of being the only reliable option, so why give that up? In short, it’s highly unlikely (if not impossible) for competitors to invade their niche. It helps that regulators aren’t incentivized to approve another identical market (it makes their job harder, and there’s no real industry progression), and potential competitors aren’t incentivized to try and compete. Competing with TASE is no better of an idea than competing with the NYSE if you had the chance. Reputation, especially of a business with a sound operating history, makes them largely immovable.

Customer Dependency: TASE has foregone the risk typical to most businesses, the risk that customers can be lost at a large scale for no good reason. TASE generates revenue from listing businesses/subscription fees for continuing to be listed. Their subscription model is incredibly simple, but that’s all that’s worth knowing. For TASE to make money, a business must be looking for equity financing for any variety of reasons (which aren’t of relevance here). Managers often forget how to manage the business without the availability of cash, and while they may do very well with it, they’re left with little choice. These leaves TASE continually generating subscription fees with expandable pricing power and minimal revenue downside, providing businesses don’t delist due to fraud or a special situation of some sort (which isn’t even nearly frequent enough to be an issue). For more detail, see the ‘Financials’ section.

Network Effects: As with any market operator, TASE works with broker-dealers. TASE’s reputation, and monopoly of their niche, means brokers have to operate with TASE to do what they’re paid for. They may go to other markets e.g. NYSE, but they would be dealing in different securities there, so it depends on what they want to sell. Due to TASE securities typically being less liquid, manually processed, and pricing set bid-ask spreads, Broker-Dealers typically receive higher commissions, which incentivizes them to deal in TASE securities. History has made it clear they care more about commission than customers, so you get a lot of ignorance there – hence the unrecognized value.

Scalability: due to the subscription model, they already have the infrastructure necessary to list/provide data services to businesses - they don’t have to set up another office, build a factory, or any other usual CAPEX. The marginal effort required is largely a matter of accumulating the right data and documents. This is significantly less than the marginal reward, which is mostly continual payments until the business ends up bust or acquired. This, essentially, is operating leverage. We’re now offered an apt explanation for their past, and likely continual, margin expansion.

The inverse nature of the bet: By investing in TASE, you’re essentially betting on private businesses being poorly managed, and so being reliant on equity financing. This means that, unlike 99.9% of other businesses (arbitrarily speaking), TASE are likely to benefit more in a depressed economy, so when businesses are more likely to need cash (there will be a slight lag). The retort I’d expect is ‘but shouldn’t that even out, as they lose out when things look/are more prosperous?’, to which I would firmly disagree. Their subscription fees will continue to hedge their revenue change. In short, they win when things look bleak, and barely lose out when things look brighter, which provides them (and so, by extension, investors) with an enticingly asymmetric corporate risk-return profile.

Financials

Note: any averages, unless otherwise stated, go back to 2019 – this is when their publicly available data goes back to.

Income Statement

TASE generates revenues mostly based on a subscription service (about 40% of revenues, based on the strictest definition), which affords TASE largely recurring revenues. Their subscription works as a typical model – investors/businesses require all the services so they have the information necessary to abide to, and make best use of, the listings. Their subsidiaries are more likely to be subject to reinvestment than not, for the sake of improvement, but the only upkeep required is updating their large information database and making sure their websites don’t crash (which has proved more than manageable). The downside of any subscription model is the trade-off between growth and stability. TASE's income statement is not likely to see any sudden increase in growth rate, but I see no issues with a slow and steady compounder. As TASE is relatively undervalued (see ‘Comps Valuation’), the growth is a bonus to our returns. I suppose, by this point, you’re probably wondering what the numbers are, so without further ado:

TASE’s revenue, as established, is positively correlated with how many businesses list/stay listed on TASE markets, albeit somewhat cyclically. New listings often follow a depressive general environment of some sort (2023 saw a 3% decrease in new listing fees, which is indicative of new listings – prices haven’t changed meaningfully. This is a return to a more normalized state). New Listings, indicating the allocation ability of private managers, has increased 10%* on average each. So, while there’s been significant volatility in listings, subscriptions are the vast majority of revenues (I’ll avoid repeating myself).It is odd that, while growth varied, there hasn’t been a down year since 2019, nor in net income (as far back as data is available, but I’d take it with a pinch of salt). The revenue increase is 10.5% CAGR, which isn’t ideal, but given the circumstances, is acceptable.

*including dual securities

TASE's 2024 operating margin was 28%. Their average is 19% (rounded), which has been pushed down due to margin expansion at 28.7% CAGR. They have been allocating capital towards reinvesting into the business. Exchange businesses tend to follow this pattern, but these reinvestments are a longer-term bet on cash generation, so it’s not worrying. Given at least a year, operating margins could return to the normalised levels (base case), or possibly peak if this speculation’s history repeats itself.

Net income followed a similar pattern - TASE has demonstrated the ability to generate satisfactory profit on their operation. Their average net margin is 15% (again pushed down), in which the trend has been satisfactory, but by no means smooth. The average net income growth is 42%, which is that operating leverage again. There have been no down years, mainly because of that too. Any business that experiences completely linear growth comes across as Enron-esque. TASE is good, but not too good to be true. If you wait long enough, something will go wrong eventually.

Balance Sheet

Liabilities

TASE's balance sheet is incredibly simple – there are very few items to consider (as is typical of exchanges).

Management has done excellently in ensuring TASE maintain low debt levels (something they don’t share with most of their peers of similar activity, who tend to attempt expansions into more niches). There has only been debt the last two years, but cash/debt has been 5.3 and 8.5 respectively. Usually, there’s more to say on debt, but it’s hard when it’s negligible. It is not entirely clear to me what point there was in taking on this debt – they could have done just fine without it.

The majority of their liabilities are loan or deferred revenue related. This is typical of exchanges, due to the subscription model.

There are no off-balance sheet arrangements or preferred shares.

Assets

Being a software-based business, their tangible asset requirements are incredibly low.

TASE, as you may have guessed, has a stable balance sheet. They’re somewhat capital intensive, which puts some pressure on their cash pile, but they have enough to cover total liabilities. Their cash pile has grown annually at 10% CAGR, which I expect to continue, especially as markets continue to become more speculative. See the Cash Flow Statement section for what they could do with this cash.

It shouldn’t come as a surprise that their non-current assets are mostly intangible, the most important being property and equipment: lease right-of-use assets, deferred tax, and IP. The proportions of these have stayed constant enough over the years to be contributed to the nature of their business model. These non-current assets are a necessity for generating a revenue, which isn’t going to change, most likely ever.

Generating and Allocating cash

TASE have paid an 8% dividend in 2024, with an average dividend coverage of 1.55. The dividend yield has varied over time in tandem with operating results, but nothing out of tune e.g. the 2024Q1-3 dividend yield was 1% (a 75% decrease from the year prior), which closely matches the decrease in the dividend payment. They pay a dividend of a size they think is appropriate, when/if required as the best use of cash. If the benefits of paying dividends are to be accrued, paying them consistently is their first port of call.

I would prefer, mostly for the tax-free nature, for repurchases to be prioritised. While repurchases have been executed when the management believes the stock is below their guess at fair value (which isn’t always, necessarily, accurate), that hasn’t been consistent YoY. There has been net repurchases, with shares outstanding decreasing 10% total since the 2019 IPO.

There is nothing more which reveals the value present here.

Intrinsic Value

TASE, even at an initial glance, is obviously undervalued. In terms of how much, a crude Intrinsic Value calculation yields insight.

My assumptions were as follows:

So, we have a likely 2-4 bagger staring us in the face. There’s no specified timeline, which is the main issue with value acting as its own catalyst. The market could be ignorant for a week, but it could also be ignorant for a lifetime, so let’s not waste any more time thinking about it. I’m not in favour of this condition, hence the applied margin of safety, but it’s worth taking with a pinch of salt. You can only think about so much and remain effective, and this doesn’t fit the mental budget in the slightest.

Comp Valuation

TASE, based on its P/FCF, is undervalued relative to its peers. Considering cash flows are all that drives stock prices in the long run (provided the absence of long periods of mania, which is always possible), this is appealing. So, while there’s some variation, it’s much more to do with that country’s economic situation than anything else.

TASE's P/FCF is 25. If they reach the industry average (which they should eventually, if market history upholds), we’re presented with a 64% upside*. I prefer using an industry average - it eliminates having to think about if each business considered is over/under valued, which can be done at a glance, but to no accurate extent. The less considered the better - there are less chances for mistakes that way.

I’d take into account, when looking at things yourself, the fact that some of these firms trading well above the average are, of course, overvalued (hence our using the average). Whether you prefer using intrinsic value or relative multiples, the opportunity is clear enough anyway. If you are trying to come to a decision, my advice is this: use a relative multiple. Intrinsic value runs on more assumptions, and making more decisions complicates things. Superior investment returns are earned by keeping things largely simple – you can assess something much better when you only have to think about a few things. It does not get any riskier either way, at least on a price basis, but we must minimize the real source of risk: poor consideration of our investments.

*Note: I cannot give a timeline on when this value will be realized – people are too unpredictable, but the opportunity is not diminished.

Management

Judging by the moat (qualitative and quantitative) TASE has developed, it’s apparent their management is broadly effective (the management composition hasn’t really changed as far back as we look). They’re critical thinkers and honest people – they address their mistakes first, and more importantly, how they came about, and what’s being done about them. While looking for management characteristics is always a very personal affair, there are traits which anyone should agree with.

The management is granted stock options, which is a crucial tell. The manager/shareholder interests must be aligned. If they’re not, it is highly likely shareholders will lose out before any value is realized. There are many ways a business can go, so we must make sure objectives are aligned. If they weren’t, I’d avoid the stock. If I get any inkling that this is starting to change, I will issue an update immediately.

There’s not been much change in board composition recently, which is another positive tell. In order for management to run a business well, they must immerse themselves in it for a long period of time. They have to understand it like they would a person with all their complications. The management here has clearly done that, hence the exceptional state of TASE.

Risks

IPOs slowing: If IPOs were too slow, TASE would forego listing fee revenue, which is consistently of at least mild importance. I say ‘mild’ because, per 2024Q3 data, listing fees are averaging 21% of revenues, which makes a material difference to the cash flow generation of the business. This is not a large risk– businesses, as a class, will always be poorly managed, and otherwise they wouldn’t need to list in larger flocks each economic cycle. If this ever changes, the investment environment is completely changed, and all investing ceases to exist. History has made it clear this risk will stay mild (see new listing figures, income statement section) – the world is far too competitive and fast-paced. Stock exchanges are unusual businesses in the sense that their environment doesn’t really change at all to any meaningful extent, so they largely forego this risk.

There are c.700 listed businesses on the TASE, which is relatively low compared to comps. This, to a fair extent, concentrates their bets. You could say that there’s enough business listed to be diversified, but that disregards the fact that businesses often move as a class. This means that, there is a slight risk, businesses have a change of heart. However, as was made clear in the moat section, there is little reason this risk would materialize. Even if it did, the value change would likely be negligible.

Regulation: The problem with regulation is that, while timely announcements and news coverage dress it up as an orderly affair, it’s really one big mess. Looking at the history of exchanges and businesses within them, you’d quickly realize that there isn’t always the most justifiable reason for these sudden changes. My favorite example of this is how it, solely for smaller businesses, (due to low op costs) can be cheaper to violate regulations than pay most some of the fees (which do vary, and can bankrupt them if they continually do this). If there are slow changes, TASE’s management has enough time to figure out their first port of call to protect themselves, but the same could not be said for the sudden ones. This may lead to TASE having to restrict some activities/be more in depth in others, which would change the long-term composition of their revenues. If this occurs, the hedge they get from subscriptions being most of their revenue may dwindle, and their moat eroded. Due to its unpredictable nature, I can’t provide specifics, but the idea is that their top/bottom line will be challenged. This would harm their CF generating ability, which may slow down TASE being recognized for its merits, and erode our value along the way.

Note: I excluded risks that aren’t idiosyncratic to the business - they don’t bear on TASE’s merits. There’s little point in devoting them any thought beyond deciding to ignore them – they are too unpredictable and generalized.

Approach for Purchase

My approach is to buy as long as it's relatively cheap on a P/FCF basis compared to its peers, and there’s no dissuading material changes. If it reached the industry average, I’d revaluate the opportunity at hand, but that wouldn’t necessarily dissuade me of the business’s merits (I’d be more likely to focus on intrinsic value after that). If it drops, I’d continue to buy the stock up until I feel my other positions are too diluted, which wouldn’t take long (if I have an idea I like, the only sensical option is to act on it in proportion to how appealing it is). I have no other price targets or deadlines in mind – I prefer to keep things simple, and I’m happy to hold this for as long as I’m investing in general (providing no adverse material changes). It’s very obviously undervalued, and there’s enough margin to forego the need for a specific target price, no matter what investment case you use.

Other Points of interest

Avg 3-month Daily Volume: 18724619 ($5.3mn) (volume is listed in Ila, so currency conversions were done on an approximate basis).

Idea Suitability: PA or small fund managers

Mutual Funds (c.51) will have it as part of an index of some sort, in which something like Old Westbury Small & Mid-Cap Strategies Fund stands out as a good general representation. Their institutional ownership stands at 35% (per Yahoo!)

Analyst coverage of any depth is limited, which is mainly due to a $1.24bn mkt cap. It’s just not enticing enough for the ‘analysts’ out there – TASE wouldn’t write them the check (their focus remains on value creation).

Annual Report release is in March (dates vary largely within the month), which may help clear the picture for the rest of the market. They also offer weekly, monthly, quarterly, and annual ‘reviews’, which I recommend anyone read to gain an understanding of the merits of their business. It will not take you long.

I don’t hold a position in TASE, nor am I affiliated with the company in any way through employment or communications.

Catalyst

Value can act as its own catalyst, and in most cases it does (whether it is direct or indirect is another matter entirely). The market will eventually recognize the mistake they have made (and if it doesn’t, value investing needs a rethink). It should become more obvious to them as free cash flows (AKA value creation) outpace prices, but until then, our opportunity will expand with time.

Market review

The S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) gained 2.41% during the fourth quarter of 2024. Led once again by mega-cap, technology-focused companies, U.S. equities moved higher over the removal of the U.S. election uncertainty, solid economic growth, lower rates, healthy corporate earnings, and expectations of a more business-friendly regulatory environment. West Texas Intermediate crude oil prices rose 5%, U.S. large caps outperformed small caps, and growth outperformed value.

Persistently high shelter costs pushed November’s annualized rate of U.S. headline Consumer Price Index (CPI) inflation back up to +2.7%, while annualized core CPI inflation held steady at 3.3%. New U.S. job growth rebounded in November, while the unemployment rate ticked up to 4.2%. U.S. annualized real gross domestic product (GDP) expanded by 3.1% during the third quarter of 2024. The consensus estimates for fourth quarter real GDP growth are now ~3%, reflecting growing confidence in the health of the U.S. economy.

In December, the Federal Open Market Committee reduced the federal funds rate by another 25 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%), down to a target range of between 4.25% and 4.50%. However, in an increasingly hawkish shift, the U.S. central bank projected only two additional 25-bp rate cuts in 2025, lowering the federal funds rate to approximately 3.90% by the end of 2025.

Portfolio Positioning: Quarterly Attribution and Analysis

Fund characteristics included: 50 holdings, a target sector weight +/-5% relative to the benchmark, smaller average market cap versus the S&P 500 Index, and identification of attractively valued companies with improving earnings fundamentals relative to the benchmark. The fund returned 5.04% for the quarter, while the benchmark S&P 500 Index returned 2.41%. The fund’s outperformance was attributed primarily to positive stock selection within the industrials, financials, and health care sectors. Negative stock selection within the consumer discretionary and energy sectors detracted from performance. United Airlines (UAL) is a major U.S. airline carrier that is more focused on international and long-haul travel relative to its large U.S. peers. United Airlines delivered third quarter earnings ahead of consensus estimates, provided encouraging forward guidance, and announced a new share repurchase program. The solid results were driven by easing fuel prices and an improving domestic revenue environment. Although discount airlines are struggling, United has been opportunistic post-pandemic, focusing on international service, premium products, and scale. United offers the best in-flight connectivity in the industry, which is a differentiator for higher-yielding corporate travelers. United Airlines has remained attractively valued and has maintained a solid balance sheet, and management is well positioned to deploy capital for further share buybacks. PulteGroup (PHM), one of the largest homebuilders in the U.S., reported third quarter revenue and earnings ahead of consensus estimates. However, shares sold off over Pulte’s lower-gross-margin guide for the fourth quarter that highlighted concerns that elevated incentives will affect margins amid an affordability-challenged environment. Pulte has remained attractively valued and has maintained a solid balance sheet and peer-leading margins. We are confident Pulte’s management team should continue executing and capitalize on the secular trends favoring home ownership.

Over the past 12 months, the fund returned 26.29% while the S&P 500 Index returned 25.02%. The outperformance was attributed primarily to positive stock selection within the financials, industrials, and health care sectors. Negative stock selection within the information technology, energy, and materials sectors detracted from performance. The fund’s modest overweight to consumer discretionary, energy, and health care also detracted from relative performance. Over the longer term, broad diversification and risk controls surrounding sector weights typically have resulted in stock selection being the main driver of performance within the strategy. EMCOR Group, Inc. (EME), is a specialty contractor and provider of electrical and mechanical construction and facilities services, building services, and industrial services. In 2024, EMCOR consistently delivered quarterly results ahead of expectations and raised guidance. Management noted strong demand and backlog from energy efficiency and data center projects, network communications, and a reshoring of industrial and semiconductor manufacturing. EMCOR has maintained a stable balance sheet, has been returning free cash flow to shareholders, and has become the contractor of choice within the nonresidential construction market. AGCO Corp. (AGCO) is a global leader in agricultural farming equipment and related replacement parts, with strong positioning in both the European and South American markets. In July, AGCO reported quarterly results that missed consensus estimates, with revenue and margins well below expectations. Management cut earnings guidance by ~33%, citing lower commodity prices and ongoing challenges in South America. The fund sold off its position in AGCO due to deteriorating model ranks and an elevated risk of future earnings disappointments.

Portfolio Positioning: Outlook

As we enter 2025, it appears the U.S. has achieved a soft economic landing. Continued disinflation, lower interest rates, and a strong labor market have eliminated most near-term U.S. recession risks. In September 2024, the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) cut rates for the first time since the 2020 onset of the COVID-19 pandemic, concluding the Fed’s fastest tightening cycle in more than four decades. However, the persistence of inflation, strong economic growth, and low unemployment should force the U.S. central bank to take a more measured approach in 2025. Despite the looming threat of a tariff war by the incoming Trump administration, U.S. GDP growth will likely continue outperforming other developed markets, as monetary policy may shift rapidly from a significant headwind into an economic tailwind.

Since mid-July, equity market returns have broadened out from the “Magnificent Seven” (Alphabet, Amazon, Apple, Meta, Microsoft, NVIDIA, and Tesla), as the “other 493 stocks” have performed relatively well. After the concentrated mega-cap rally of 2023 and throughout most of 2024, equity markets in 2025 are likely to be defined by slowing global economic growth, single-digit earnings growth, and a broadening of returns. We think that volatility is likely amid a looming tariff war and ongoing geopolitical tensions surrounding Ukraine, the Middle East, and Taiwan. Given this backdrop, we expect 2025 to provide opportunities for disciplined stock selection to add value as it did during 2024. We currently anticipate volatile and range-bound markets and are mindful to neutralize our exposures to macroeconomic and geopolitical risks where possible. While we are generally cautious, there are potential positive catalysts from decelerating inflation, lower rates, a resilient U.S. job market, improving corporate earnings, and a broadening of market returns.
